ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic croissant entry (FOODON_03301521); no specific almond croissant subtype exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers croissants broadly; no almond-filled variant code exists. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for almond croissant or croissant pastry as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard almond croissant weighing approximately 90 g, as typically served at a bakery or café.

nutrient_source.title: Almond Croissant — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F11110001 is sourced directly from HPB Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from the HPB record without modification. Saturated_fat_g and trans_fat_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy butter composition data as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for almond croissant in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 67 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: croissant (plain, white flour, baked), GI ~67 (medium), as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as GI × available carbohydrate per serving / 100; for a 90 g serving with available carbohydrate approximately 25 g net after fibre adjustment, GL = 67 × 25 / 100 ≈ 16.8. The frangipane filling and sliced almond topping add fat and protein that moderately blunt the glycaemic response. Estimation basis documented per Veritas policy.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (304 mg/100 g), high saturated fat (~13.5 g/100 g), and high sugar (17.1 g/100 g) with minimal potassium or fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit almond croissants: sodium at 304 mg/100 g contributes meaningfully to daily sodium load, saturated fat promotes arterial stiffness, and the high sugar content with low fibre (2.5 g/100 g) further elevates cardiovascular risk; if consumed, limit to a half portion occasionally and pair with a potassium-rich food such as banana or spinach.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar (17.1 g/100 g exceeding the 15 g/100 g threshold) and high refined carbohydrate (39.3 g/100 g) with only 2.5 g/100 g dietary fibre, producing a rapid postprandial glucose spike
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid almond croissants: the combination of high sugar from frangipane filling, refined flour base, and inadequate fibre creates a high glycaemic load that is difficult to manage; if occasionally consumed, limit to a half portion and pair with a protein source such as eggs to blunt the glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~13.5 g/100 g from butter), estimated trans fat (0.2 g/100 g from butter), high sugar (17.1 g/100 g), and high total fat (28.55 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat almond croissants as an occasional indulgence only: saturated fat at approximately 13.5 g/100 g substantially exceeds the recommended per-meal limit, trans fat from butter is pro-atherogenic at any amount, and the high sugar content promotes dyslipidaemia and endothelial inflammation; a single 90 g croissant delivers roughly 12.2 g saturated fat, far exceeding the per-meal limit for CVD patients.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat (~13.5 g/100 g from butter, well above the 5 g/100 g RA gate) and refined sugar (17.1 g/100 g) promote systemic inflammation and cytokine activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid almond croissants: butter-derived saturated fat at approximately 13.5 g/100 g does not qualify for the MUFA/PUFA anti-inflammatory carve-out and actively promotes inflammatory pathways, while the high sugar content further elevates pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; the sliced almond topping provides negligible anti-inflammatory benefit relative to the overall pro-inflammatory profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: An almond croissant is a buttery, flaky French pastry made from laminated yeast dough enriched with generous layers of butter, filled with a sweet almond cream known as frangipane (a mixture of ground almonds, butter, sugar, and eggs), and finished with a topping of sliced almonds that toast golden during baking. The result is a pastry with a shatteringly crisp, layered exterior, a soft and slightly chewy interior, and a rich, marzipan-like sweetness from the almond filling. Almond croissants are widely sold in bakeries and cafés across Singapore and internationally, typically consumed at breakfast or as a mid-morning snack. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion delivers 451 kcal, 28.6 g fat (of which approximately 13.5 g is saturated fat from butter), 39.3 g carbohydrate, 17.1 g sugar, 9.3 g protein, and 304 mg sodium. The high saturated fat, high sugar, and moderate-to-high glycaemic index (estimated GI ~67, medium) make this pastry a high-concern food for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The frangipane filling compounds the glycaemic load beyond what the plain croissant base alone would produce. Clinically, this food should be treated as an occasional indulgence rather than a regular dietary item for any health-conscious consumer.
common_user_queries: How many calories in an almond croissant?
Is almond croissant high in sugar?
Is almond croissant suitable for hypertension?
Is almond croissant high in saturated fat?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11110001, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). The source is an approved primary regional source. Saturated fat and trans fat are estimated from standard butter composition data cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (butter, salted, USDA SR Legacy) given that HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this item. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (plain croissant, GI 67).
notes: G3 DOCUMENTATION — Fields set to -1 and basis for unavailability: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are all set to -1 because detailed fatty acid fractions, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for almond croissant are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for F11110001, and no sufficiently close match for an almond-filled frangipane croissant was found in USDA FoodData Central (searched April 2026) that would provide these values with defensible provenance. Sources consulted and confirmed unavailable: HPB Foodo (F11110001, accessed 2026-04-16) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods (searched for croissant, almond croissant, frangipane pastry, accessed 2026-04-16). These fields should be updated when a laboratory analysis or a directly matching approved-source entry becomes available. ESTIMATION NOTES — saturated_fat_g (13.5 g/100 g) is estimated from standard butter composition (USDA SR Legacy: butter, salted, NDB 01001, saturated fat ~51% of total fat) applied to the total fat of 28.55 g/100 g, consistent with a butter-dominant laminated pastry; this is an approximation and should be replaced with a direct HPB or laboratory measurement when available. trans_fat_g (0.2 g/100 g) is estimated from naturally occurring trans fat in butter (typically 2–5% of butter fat); this is a conservative lower-bound estimate. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (plain croissant); no direct measurement for almond croissant exists in any approved GI source as of 2026-04-16. ONTOLOGY — FoodOn FOODON_03301521 and FoodEx2 A00LC are close matches for croissant; no almond croissant-specific codes exist in either system. MULTILINGUAL — local names are not applicable: almond croissant is a French-origin pastry with no established Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese native-language name requiring mandatory coverage under Veritas multilingual rules. HALAL — halal status is assumed based on typical bakery ingredients; individual product halal certification should be verified.

Iteration 2: Updated ontology match_type values from 'close' to 'closest_parent'; added -1 field documentation to notes section.

Iteration 3: Expanded notes section to explicitly document every -1 field with (a) the field name, (b) the reason data is unavailable, and (c) the specific approved sources consulted that confirmed unavailability (HPB Foodo F11110001 and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods, both accessed 2026-04-16). This directly resolves all 19 G3 numeric hygiene failures reported by the validator in iteration 2.
